title: Classifier Evaluation Metrics
overview: Covers classifier evaluation from IB031: the confusion matrix, accuracy and its failure on imbalanced classes, precision, recall, F1 score, specificity and FPR, multi-class generalisation, the ROC curve, and ROC-AUC with its probabilistic interpretation.
creator: ib031-study

cards.question: A test predicts 'cancer' for a patient who does not have cancer. This is called a:
options: True positive (TP)
False negative (FN)
False positive (FP)
True negative (TN)
cards.explanation: FP (false positive / type I error / false alarm): the classifier predicts the positive class (1) but the true class is negative (0). TN: correctly predicted negative. TP: correctly predicted positive. FN: predicted negative but actually positive.

cards.prompt: Accuracy = (TP + TN) / (P + N), where P = TP + FN is the number of ___ examples and N = TN + FP is the number of ___ examples.
acceptedAnswers: true positive class, true negative class
cards.explanation: P (positives) = all examples that truly belong to class 1 = TP + FN. N (negatives) = all examples that truly belong to class 0 = TN + FP. Accuracy is the fraction of all examples that were correctly classified.

cards.question: A dataset has 90 class-0 examples and 10 class-1 examples. A classifier that always predicts class 0 achieves which accuracy?
cards.explanation: TP=0, TN=90, FP=0, FN=10 → Accuracy = 90/100 = 90%. This illustrates why accuracy is misleading for imbalanced classes: a trivial classifier that ignores the minority class can look very good. Precision and Recall expose this failure.

cards.question: Precision measures:
options: How often an actually positive example is predicted positive
How often a predicted positive is actually positive
How often an actually negative example is predicted negative
The total fraction of correctly classified examples
cards.explanation: Precision = TP / PP = TP / (TP + FP). It answers: 'Of all examples the classifier called positive, what fraction truly are positive?' High precision means few false alarms. In contrast, Recall = TP / P answers: 'Of all truly positive examples, what fraction did the classifier catch?'

cards.question: In a cancer screening application, which metric is generally more important to maximise, and why?
options: Precision, because false positives waste medical resources
Recall, because missing a real cancer case (false negative) is more dangerous than a false alarm
Accuracy, because it summarises overall performance
Specificity, because most patients are healthy
cards.explanation: A false negative (missed cancer) has severe consequences — delayed treatment. A false positive (unnecessary follow-up) is costly but not life-threatening. High recall ensures most cancers are caught. For spam detection the trade-off reverses: high precision matters more (don't discard legitimate email), recall matters less.

cards.prompt: The F1 score is the ___ mean of Precision and Recall, defined as F1 = 2TP / (2TP + FP + FN).
cards.explanation: The harmonic mean of Precision (P) and Recall (R) is 2PR/(P+R) = 2TP/(2TP+FP+FN). It is preferred over the arithmetic mean because it penalises extreme imbalance between P and R: a classifier with P=1 and R=0.01 gets F1 ≈ 0.02, not 0.5.

cards.question: Which of the following metrics are NOT used in the F1 formula?
options: TP
FP
TN
FN
cards.explanation: F1 = 2TP/(2TP+FP+FN) does not involve TN. This is a known limitation: F1 is insensitive to how well the classifier handles the negative class. If the negative class is important, the classes should be swapped and a second F1 computed.

cards.question: Sensitivity (TPR), specificity (TNR), and FPR are defined as:
options: TPR = TP/PP; TNR = TN/PN; FPR = FP/PP
TPR = TP/P; TNR = TN/N; FPR = FP/N
TPR = TP/P; TNR = TN/P; FPR = FP/P
TPR = TP/(TP+FP); TNR = TN/(TN+FN); FPR = FP/(FP+FN)
cards.explanation: TPR (sensitivity, recall) = TP/P — proportion of actual positives correctly identified. TNR (specificity) = TN/N — proportion of actual negatives correctly identified. FPR (false alarm rate) = FP/N — proportion of actual negatives incorrectly flagged. Note: FPR = 1 − TNR.

cards.question: The ROC curve plots which two quantities as the classification threshold T varies from 0 to 1?
options: Precision vs Recall
TPR vs FPR
Accuracy vs F1
TP count vs TN count
cards.explanation: ROC(T) = (TPR[T], FPR[T]). At T=0 everything is predicted positive: TPR=1, FPR=1. At T=1 everything is predicted negative: TPR=0, FPR=0. A perfect classifier can reach the top-left corner (TPR=1, FPR=0). A random classifier lies on the diagonal (AUC ≈ 0.5).

cards.question: The probabilistic interpretation of ROC-AUC is:
options: The probability that a randomly chosen example is correctly classified
The probability that a randomly chosen positive example receives a higher predicted score than a randomly chosen negative example
The expected F1 score over all possible classification thresholds
The fraction of the ROC space covered by the classifier
cards.explanation: AUC = P(h_i > h_j) where i is a random positive example and j is a random negative example. An AUC of 1.0 means the classifier perfectly ranks all positives above all negatives. AUC ≈ 0.5 is random guessing. This interpretation makes AUC threshold-independent and useful for comparing classifiers.

cards.question: In a multi-class confusion matrix with classes {big, medium, small}, M_{ij} counts examples of class i classified as class j. The overall accuracy is:
options: The sum of all off-diagonal entries divided by the total
The sum of diagonal entries M_{ii} divided by the total number of examples
The average of per-class precisions
The maximum per-class F1 score
cards.explanation: Accuracy = (Σ_i M_{ii}) / M_{••} where M_{••} is the total count of all examples. The diagonal entries are the correctly classified examples. In the multi-class case, per-class Precision[i] = M_{ii} / M_{•i} (column sum) and Recall[i] = M_{ii} / M_{i•} (row sum).

cards.question: A colleague reports 92% accuracy on a disease detection task. Why might this be misleading, and what metrics would give a more complete picture?
cards.explanation: If only 8% of patients have the disease, a classifier that always predicts 'healthy' achieves 92% accuracy — while being completely useless for the task. Precision and Recall expose this: such a classifier has Recall=0 (catches no sick patients). In medical screening, Recall (sensitivity) is crucial — missing a true case has serious consequences. Specificity (TNR) also matters to avoid overloading follow-up systems. ROC-AUC is useful for comparing probabilistic classifiers threshold-independently. F1 can summarise both Precision and Recall in a single number, though it ignores TN.
